title: Automated Building Information Modeling Compliance Check through a Large Language Model Combined with Deep Learning and Ontology
contribution: 结合LLM、深度学习和本体知识模型实现自动化BIM合规检查，显著减少手动干预和标注需求
pdfFile: Chen et al. - 2024 - Automated Building Information Modeling Compliance Check through a Large Language Model Combined wit.pdf
markdownFile: Chen et al. - 2024 - Automated Building Information Modeling Compliance Check through a Large Language Model Combined wit.md
abstract: Ensuring compliance with complex industry standards and regulations during the design and implementation phases of construction projects is a significant challenge in the building information modeling (BIM) domain. Traditional manual compliance checking methods are inefficient and error-prone, failing to meet modern engineering demands. Natural language processing (NLP) and deep learning methods have improved efficiency and accuracy in rule interpretation and compliance checking. However, these methods still require extensive manual feature engineering, large annotated datasets, and significant computational resources. Large language models (LLMs) provide robust language understanding with minimal labeled data due to their pre-training and few-shot learning capabilities. However, their application in the AEC field is still limited by the need for fine-tuning for specific tasks and handling complex texts with nested clauses and conditional statements. This study introduces an innovative automated compliance checking framework that integrates LLM, deep learning models, and ontology knowledge models. The use of LLM is motivated by its few-shot learning capability, which significantly reduces the need for large annotated datasets required by previous methods. Deep learning is employed to preliminarily classify regulatory texts, which further enhances the accuracy of structured information extraction by the LLM compared to directly feeding raw data into the LLM. This novel combination of deep learning and LLM significantly enhances the efficiency and accuracy of compliance checks by automating the processing of regulatory texts and reducing manual intervention.
methodology: A four-component automated compliance checking framework: (1) Construction of a lightweight domain ontology model using Protégé based on Chinese Residential Design Code GB-50096-2011, with classes, data attributes, and object attributes for residential building design; (2) Preliminary classification of regulatory texts into single-layer, double-layer, and triple-layer information categories using a deep learning fusion model combining BERT (Chinese-MacBERT-base), TextCNN, and LSTM with a Transformer encoder layer; (3) Structured information extraction using GPT-4 with one-shot in-context learning (ICL), guided by pre-classification labels and designed prompts (Object, Property, Condition, Value labels), temperature set to 0; (4) Compliance checking via SPARQL queries executed against the ontology model, converting LLM-extracted structured information into rule-based queries to identify non-compliant BIM objects. BIM model data (IFC format) is mapped to RDF and integrated into the ontology model for querying.
results: The proposed BERT-TextCNN-LSTM fusion model achieved the best text classification performance with 0.97 validation accuracy, outperforming individual models (TextCNN, LSTM, BERT) across all three information layer categories (F1-scores: 0.995 for single-layer, 0.951 for double-layer, 0.954 for triple-layer). GPT-4 with one-shot ICL successfully extracted structured information (Object-Property-Condition-Value) from regulatory texts across all complexity levels. The automated SPARQL-based compliance checking correctly identified non-compliant BIM objects in a nine-story residential building case study, locating specific floors and rooms that violated area requirements from GB-50096-2011. The framework demonstrated high efficiency in processing complex multi-layer regulatory clauses, significantly reducing manual intervention compared to traditional compliance checking methods.
